title: Breakdown of Thermalization from Real-Time Dynamics in the Two-Dimensional Hubbard Model
authors: Alessandro Sinibaldi, Luciano Loris Viteritti, Riccardo Rende, Fakher F. Assaad, Giuseppe Carleo
abstract: Thermalization in strongly correlated fermionic systems remains a central open problem in quantum many-body physics. In this work, we investigate the real-time dynamics and the approach to thermalization in the two-dimensional Hubbard model, a paradigmatic framework for correlated electrons, relevant to high-temperature superconductivity and ultracold quantum simulation. Focusing on the half-filled square lattice, we monitor the time evolution of the double occupancy following a quench in the on-site interaction $U$, and assess whether its long-time value is captured by a canonical thermal ensemble. We employ time-dependent variational Monte Carlo methods combined with transformer-based Neural-Network Quantum States to accurately describe the nonequilibrium dynamics of fermions, especially for the behavior at long times, thereby accessing regimes that were previously inaccessible to numerical simulations. Our results reveal two distinct dynamical behaviors: for weak to intermediate interactions, the long-time double occupancy agrees with the thermal prediction, consistent with ergodic relaxation; beyond a critical interaction $U_{C}$, the dynamics deviate markedly from the thermal expectation, revealing clear signatures of thermalization breaking. These results establish numerical simulation as a powerful tool to probe nonequilibrium quantum phenomena in correlated fermionic matter.
summary: The paper investigates how strongly correlated electrons in the 2D Hubbard model approach equilibrium after a sudden change in interaction strength. Using advanced Neural Network Quantum States within tVMC simulations, the authors find that while thermalization occurs at weak coupling, strong interactions cause the system dynamics to fundamentally deviate from expected thermal behavior, signaling a breakdown of ergodicity.
main_problem: The study investigates whether thermalization occurs during the real-time evolution of a strongly correlated fermionic system after being quenched out of equilibrium.
main_result: The dynamics exhibit two regimes: for weak interactions, the system thermalizes; however, beyond a critical interaction strength ($U_C$), the time evolution deviates significantly from thermal predictions, indicating a breakdown of thermalization.
method: Time-dependent Variational Monte Carlo (tVMC) methods are employed, utilizing Neural-Network Quantum States (NQS) based on transformer architectures to simulate non-equilibrium dynamics. The infinite-time limit is extracted using the Linear Variational Method (LVM).
model_or_system: The system modeled is the two-dimensional Hubbard model, a canonical framework for studying correlated electrons relevant to high-temperature superconductivity.
key_observables: Double occupancy ($\langle\hat{d}angle(t)$), comparison between long-time dynamic values ($\langle\hat{d}angle_\infty$) and thermal ensemble predictions ($\langle\hat{d}angle_{	ext{th}}$).
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($U$), critical interaction $U_C$, lattice size (2D square lattice), and simulation time/ramping protocol.
main_assumptions: The use of tVMC with NQS provides an accurate description of the non-equilibrium dynamics, and LVM is used to extrapolate the long-time limit.
figures_summary: Figures compare the time evolution of double occupancy ($\langle\hat{d}angle(t)$) for varying interaction strengths ($U$), contrasting tVMC results against thermal expectations and showing clear deviations above a critical $U$.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the physical problem (thermalization in Hubbard model), details the non-equilibrium protocol (interaction quench), presents advanced computational methods (tVMC/NQS/LVM), analyzes key observables ($\langle\hat{d}angle$), and concludes by mapping out the phase diagram regarding thermalization breakdown.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses fundamental questions in non-equilibrium quantum dynamics, which is crucial for understanding energy relaxation and transport properties in materials like cuprate superconductors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic illustration of the dynamical protocol studied to investigate thermalization. An initial Fermi sea state is evolved using the Hubbard Hamiltonian in Eq. (1) with the interaction strength U(t) following a linear ramp quench with final interaction U. We expect that for U smaller than a critical value UC the double occupancy ⟨ˆd⟩(t) [see Eq. (3)] at long time relaxes to the value predicted by thermalization, whereas for U larger than UC it departs from the thermal prediction.

Figure 2. Panel (a): Time evolution of the double occupancy ⟨ˆd⟩(t) [see Eq. (3)] for various interaction strengths U on a 8 × 8 lattice. The empty markers denote the tVMC simulations, while dashed lines indicate the dynamics extended via the time-dependent LVM. The shaded region highlights the time interval from which tVMC states are selected to perform the LVM computation. The arrows show the infinite-time limit of the observable from LVM. The Monte Carlo errors are smaller than the markers and are not shown for clarity. Panel (b): Infinite-time value of the double occupancy ⟨ˆd⟩∞compared with the effective thermal value ⟨ˆd⟩th as a function of U. The inset displays the order parameter ∆d...

Figure 3. Effective temperature Teff as a function of the in- teraction strength U for the dynamics with U = 1, 3, 4, 5 shown in Fig. 2 at times t = 0 (black square) and t = τ (red crosses). The dashed lines indicate the boundaries of the finite-temperature putative phase diagram of Ref. [52]. Points at intermediate times (empty circles) are also shown for U = 5 to illustrate the trajectory through the phase diagram during the dynamics. The arrows indicate the direction of time.

Figure 5. Time evolution of the double occupancy ⟨ˆd⟩(t) at U = 3 for a one-dimensional cluster with N = 36 sites and for the N = 6 × 6 square lattice. tVMC calculations are in- dicated with empty markers. The dashed black line indicates the result obtained from MPS-TDVP simulation. The inset reports the TDVP error [see Eq. (12)] along the trajectories as a measure of the variational accuracy. The Monte Carlo errors are smaller than the markers and are not shown for clarity.

Figure 4. Time evolution with tVMC (empty markers) of the double occupancy ⟨ˆd⟩(t) with different U for the 4 × 4 lattice. The solid black lines represent the exact diagonalization (ED) results. The Monte Carlo errors are smaller than the markers and are not shown for clarity.
